steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student claims that from 1992 to 1995, Girl Scout participation peaked among a certain age group and then declined among older participants. Supporting data must show a younger age group consistently outnumbering an older age group across all four years.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Only once between 1992 and 1995 did the number of Ambassadors in the Girl Scouts exceed the number of Brownies': The table shows Ambassadors never exceeded Brownies in any year — this misreads the data. ✗
  • Choice B — 'There were 1,319,000 Brownies in the Girl Scouts in 1992 but 1,142,000 in 1995': This shows Brownies declining over time within their own age group, but does not compare younger vs. older participants to support the claim about age-based decline. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Throughout the years from 1992 to 1995, the number of Daisies in the Girl Scouts exceeded the number of Ambassadors': While plausible, the table does not support Daisies exceeding Ambassadors in every year during this period. ✗
  • Choice D — 'For each year from 1992 to 1995, there were fewer Seniors in the Girl Scouts than there were Brownies': Brownies (ages 6–8, the peak participation group) consistently outnumber Seniors (ages 14–17) by a large margin in every year — directly supporting the claim that popularity peaks young and declines with age. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
